1: David's interior monologue opens ch27: wayyōʾmer dāwiḏ ʾel libbô — 'David said in his heart.' The expression ʾāmar ʾel lēb (to say to one's heart) is the formula for inner deliberation or self-counsel — it appears in Gen 8:21 (YHWH's inner resolve after the flood), Ps 14:1 (the fool's inner denial of God), and frequently in Ecclesiastes for Qohelet's self-directed inquiry. David's inner speech is not prayer (he does not speak to YHWH) but a pragmatic calculation under pressure. That the man who just twice relied on YHWH's protection now calculates survival without mentioning YHWH is the narrative's signal of spiritual fatigue — the same exhaustion that appears in Elijah under the juniper tree (1 Kgs 19:4). The ʾāmar ʾel lēb without a divine address marks the moment when covenant confidence gives way to human calculation.
6: Achish gives David ṣiqlag (Ziklag): wayyitēn lô ʾāḵîš bayyôm hahûʾ ʾet ṣiqlag. Ziklag (ṣiqlag) is an unknown etymology — a Philistine city name with no clear Semitic derivation. The verse adds: lāḵēn hāyᵉṯāh ṣiqlag lᵉmalᵉḵê yᵉhûḏāh ʿad hayyôm hazzeh — 'therefore Ziklag has belonged to the kings of Judah until this day.' This is the narrator's anachronism that confirms the later monarchic horizon of the account: the Philistine gift becomes a Judahite royal possession, and the comment 'to this day' marks it as a living geographical memory. Ziklag is thus a type of the exile-period pattern: the anointed king receives territory in unexpected circumstances, from unexpected sources, during the period of his rejection — preparation for the throne he will eventually occupy.
2: David crosses to Gath with his six hundred men (wᵉhûʾ wᵉšeš mēʾôt ʾîš ʾimô), going to Achish son of Maoch, king of Gath. The specific identification of the Gath king as Maoch’s son (distinguishing him from the Achish of ch21 who was apparently Maor’s son, or perhaps the same person with a different patronym) reflects the narrator’s precision. The decision to go to Gath with six hundred men is a military as well as political move — David brings a credible fighting force that Achish can use, creating mutual benefit.
3: David settles (wayyēšeḇ dāwid ʿim ʾāḵîš bᵉGat) with Achish along with his men and their households — including his two wives Ahinoam and Abigail. The move of entire households signals permanence of resettlement, not merely temporary refuge. David has given up on survival in Israelite territory under Saul and is committing to a Philistine base. The inclusion of his wives marks this as full domestic transplantation, not just military alliance.
4: Saul learns that David has fled to Gath and stops searching (wayyᵉḥaḏal lᵉḇaqqēš ʾōtô). The cessation of Saul’s pursuit achieves what David sought — safety from the immediate threat. Saul’s abandonment of the pursuit is rational: David with six hundred men among the Philistines is beyond practical reach. The irony is that David’s safety comes from throwing himself on the mercy of Israel’s primary enemy — a decision that will create its own moral and political complications (ch29).
5: David’s request for a rural town: nittᵉnāh lî māqôm bᵉʾaḥat ʿārê haśśāḏeh wᵉʾēšᵉḇāh šām — give me a place in one of the towns of the field that I may settle there. The request to move out of the capital city (Gath) to a provincial town is politically shrewd: David reduces his visibility within Achish’s court while retaining the alliance. The rhetorical reason given — “why should your servant dwell in the royal city with you?” — is face-saving for Achish while serving David’s strategic purposes.
7: David’s time in Philistine territory: wᵉhāyāh mispar hayyāmîm ʾăšer yāšaḇ dāwid biśᵉḏēh pᵉlištîm yāmîm wᵉʾarbāʿāh ḥōḏāšîm — the count of days David lived in Philistine fields was days and four months, i.e., one year and four months. The term yāmîm used here as “a year” (a Hebrew idiom for an indeterminate period typically meaning a year) plus four months gives the total stay. The precision is unusual in narrative — the narrator marks the duration as significant for the chronology of David’s pre-kingship period.
8: David’s raiding policy: he raids the Geshurites, the Girzites, and the Amalekites — peoples who were yōšᵉḇê hāʾāreṣ mēʿôlām (inhabitants of the land from of old). The Geshurites (not the Geshurites of Aram-Geshur) and Girzites are archaic pre-Israelite populations in the Negev approaches; the Amalekites are Israel’s longstanding enemies (cf. 1 Sam 15). David targets populations that are both politically safe to attack (no Philistine allegiance) and theologically legitimate (traditional enemies of Israel), maintaining covenantal consistency even while serving a Philistine overlord.
9: David’s total-destruction policy: wᵉhikkāh dāwid ʾet hāʾāreṣ wᵉlōʾ yᵉḥayyeh ʾîš wᵉʾiššāh — David would strike the land and leave alive neither man nor woman. The no-survivors policy is both military — preventing intelligence reports — and consistent with the ḥerem (total destruction) principle applied to Amalekites. The spoil — sheep, oxen, donkeys, camels, clothing — is brought back to Achish. David’s application of ḥerem against Amalekites is ironic given Saul’s failure to do so (ch15), but his motivation here is strategic deception rather than covenantal obedience.
10: Achish’s question: ʾal pᵉšaṭtem hayyôm — where have you raided today? David’s deceptive answer claims raids against the Negeb of Judah, the Negeb of the Jerahmeelites, and the Negeb of the Kenites — Israelite-adjacent territories. The deception is strategic: Achish believes David is attacking his own people, making him persona non grata in Israel and permanently dependent on Philistine patronage. David’s multilevel deception — raiding Israel’s enemies while claiming to raid Israel’s allies — is narratively presented as clever survival, not moral exemplar.
11: The no-survivors policy serves intelligence security: wᵉdāwid lōʾ yᵉḥayyeh ʾîš wᵉʾiššāh lᵉhāḇîʾ Gat lēʾmōr pen yagîḏû ʿālênû lēʾmōr kōh ʿāśāh dāwid — David left no man or woman alive to bring back to Gath, saying they would tell what David had really done. The narrator explicitly provides David’s reasoning, making the deception transparent to the reader even while Achish remains deceived. The phrase wᵉḵēn miš pāṭô kol hayyāmîm ʾăšer yāšaḇ biśᵉḏēh pᵉlištîm — and this was his practice all the days he lived in Philistine territory — shows the systematic nature of the deception over sixteen months.
12: Achish’s complete trust in David: wayyaʾămēn ʾāḵîš bᵉdāwid lēʾmōr haḇʾēš hibʾîš bᵉʿammô bᵉyiśrāʾēl — Achish trusted David, thinking David had made himself utterly repulsive to his own people in Israel. The verb baʾš (to stink, to make oneself odious) is used of social excommunication — the same root used when Israel “became odious” to the Philistines in ch13:4. Achish believes David has permanently burned his bridges with Israel, becoming a “servant forever” (lî lᵉʿeḇed ʿôlām). The irony is complete: the Philistine king trusts the man who has been systematically deceiving him, while believing the man whose loyalty is entirely to YHWH and Israel.
